headlineSummary: OpenAI and Anthropic are in a close tie with mixed-neutral sentiment, while Google Gemini skews positive on limited evidence [E1][E7][E8].

ranking.summary: OpenAI reads as mixed-neutral: praise for GPT-5.2 and ChatGPT is offset by criticism of its scientific framing and safety choices, with a close tie to Anthropic [E1][E2][E3][E4][E5][E6].
Anthropic is mixed-neutral on limited evidence, with Claude Code getting a compliment overall but its TUI called out as stupid, and it is in a close tie with OpenAI [E8].
Google Gemini is positive on limited evidence, with a commenter saying its spatial understanding was critical to completing the task [E7].

evidence.excerpt: GPT-5.2 derives a new result in theoretical physics

annotations.rationale: The title attributes a notable scientific achievement to GPT-5.2, framing the model as capable and impressive.

evidence.excerpt: Like with the novel solutions GPT 5.2 has been able to find for erdos problems - many users here (even in this very thread!) think they know more about this than Fields medalist Terence Tao, who maintains this list showing that, yes, LLMs have driven these proofs:

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly praises GPT 5.2 for finding novel solutions and endorses its contributions to proofs.

evidence.excerpt: I'm willing to (begrudgingly) admit the possibility for AI to do novel work, but this particular result does not seem very impressive. I picture ChatGPT as the rich kid whose parents privately donated to a lab to get their name on a paper for college admissions. In this case, I don't think I'm being too cynical in thinking that something similar is happening here and that the role of AI in this result is being well overplayed.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly names ChatGPT and strongly dismisses the result as unimpressive and overhyped.

evidence.excerpt: OpenAI has deleted the word 'safely' from its mission

annotations.rationale: The title presents OpenAI as removing safety language from its mission, a negative framing of its priorities.

evidence.excerpt: > OpenAI said it will stop assessing its AI models prior to releasing them for the risk that they could persuade or manipulate people, possibly helping to swing elections or create highly effective propaganda campaigns. > The company said it would now address those risks through its terms of service, restricting the use of its AI models in political campaigns and lobbying, and monitoring how people are using the models once they are released for signs of violations. To see persuasion/manipulation as simply a multiplier on other invention capabilities, and something that can be patched on a model already in use, is a very specific statement on what AI safety means.

annotations.rationale: The commenter criticizes OpenAI for dropping pre-release evaluation of persuasion/manipulation risks and frames that as a serious safety failure.

evidence.excerpt: I asked ChatGPT. Not surprisingly, it knew a lot of the various tapes, file formats, sizes, processing, storage, and after it asked some clarifying questions, it was quite optimistic about me being able to do this myself

annotations.rationale: The excerpt describes ChatGPT as knowledgeable and helpful for a technical personal project, which is clear praise.

evidence.excerpt: Gemini's spatial understanding was critical in completing the task.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly credits Gemini's spatial understanding as critical to finishing the task, strong praise of capability.

evidence.excerpt: I love Claude Code, but how they made the TUI is just plain stupid.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly praises Claude Code overall but criticizes its TUI implementation, yielding mixed sentiment.
